Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Methods states the cac3 mutant used was SALK_207356, and Results names this T-DNA line cac3-1.

Source_Species.reason: The study is in Arabidopsis; CAC3 mutants are Arabidopsis lines.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper repeatedly refers to Arabidopsis; full species name Arabidopsis thaliana is directly inferable for these ABRC/SALK lines.

Applied_Species.reason: CAC3 was tested in Arabidopsis plants.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Applied species is Arabidopsis thaliana as used throughout the paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Text states CAC3 encodes the α-subunit / α-CT subunit of plastid heteromeric ACCase.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: CAC3 is described as a subunit of plastid heteromeric ACCase, supporting plastid/chloroplast localization.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results state T-DNA insertion in the 3′ UTR of CAC3 significantly reduced expression from neighboring sequences but not the coding region.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: CAC3 is used as supporting evidence for ACCase involvement and heat-sensitive dwarfism, but the paper does not directly measure C18:1/C18:2 in cac3 mutants.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: The linked end products discussed for ACCase in this study are fatty acids.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper states unsaturated fatty acids are important for membrane integrity/homeostasis and heat tolerance.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper discusses membrane integrity in leaves/chloroplasts generally, but does not directly specify CAC3-linked metabolite accumulation site.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show cac3 mutants are near wild type at 22°C but dwarf at 28°C, supporting a role of plastid ACCase in heat tolerance; membrane homeostasis is part of the broader study conclusion.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: CAC3 phenotypes were tested at 28°C heat stress.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The paper does not report a direct physical interaction between CAC3 and AccD; they are components of the same ACCase complex but no interaction assay is shown.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Results describe T-DNA mutant cac3-1, CRISPR-Cas9 mutant cac3-2, and heat-stress phenotyping.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a plausible implication, but such engineering application for CAC3 is not directly stated or tested in the paper.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each listed point matches the Results/Introduction: CAC3 encodes the α-CT subunit, cac3 mutants are heat-sensitive dwarf but near WT at 22°C, and this supports plastid ACCase involvement in heat sensitivity.

Other_Important_Info.reason: CAC3 indeed serves mainly as supporting genetic evidence for ACCase involvement rather than the main molecular focus.

corrections.reason: The paper does not report a direct physical interaction between CAC3 and AccD; they are components of the same ACCase complex but no interaction assay is shown.

Source_Species.reason: accD studied here is the Arabidopsis chloroplast gene.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The study organism is Arabidopsis thaliana.

Applied_Species.reason: All functional tests were performed in Arabidopsis.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper states ACCase catalyzes the committed step of de novo fatty acid biosynthesis and accD encodes an ACCase subunit.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: accD is explicitly part of the plastid heteromeric ACCase.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The paper explicitly says ACCase catalyzes the committed/initial rate-limiting step of fatty acid synthesis.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The text states accD encodes the β-carboxyl transferase subunit of acetyl-CoA carboxylase and is chloroplast encoded.

Enzyme_Name.reason: This wording is directly supported by the description of AccD as the β-carboxyl transferase subunit of ACCase.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number appears in the paper.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper states ACCase converts acetyl-CoA to malonyl-CoA in the initial committed step; accD encodes the β-CT subunit of this enzyme.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Acetyl-CoA is explicitly named as the substrate converted by ACCase.

Primary_Product.reason: Malonyl-CoA is explicitly named as the product of ACCase and was used in rescue experiments.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results directly show lower C18:1 and C18:2 in rare1 (accD editing-defective) and higher C18:1/C18:2 in edited accD-OE plants.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: These terminal products are fatty acids/unsaturated fatty acids.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: accD/ACCase supplies malonyl-CoA precursor for fatty acid biosynthesis, directly linking to end products.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: This summarizes the central Results: edited accD enhances heat tolerance and C18:1/C18:2, while loss of editing lowers ACCase activity, reduces these UFAs, damages membranes, and causes yellow dwarf heat-sensitive plants.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper calls ACCase the initial rate-limiting/committed step of FA synthesis.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The study states these UFAs maintain membrane structural homeostasis/fluidity and contribute to heat tolerance.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: FA changes were measured in leaves/seed contexts, and membrane effects were examined in leaf chloroplast/plasma membranes; seeds are explicitly mentioned for OE FA content.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results show heat treatment increased accD-C794 editing rate and analyzed overexpression lines under heat stress.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The paper shows accD C794 editing is heat responsive, increasing with heat-treatment time.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Results describe a construct under control of the CaMV 35S promoter for edited accD overexpression.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: All three treatments are reported: 28°C heat stress, exogenous malonyl-CoA, and exogenous C18:1/C18:2.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: accD is a chloroplast gene encoding a plastid ACCase subunit; overexpression construct also included a chloroplast transit peptide.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: RARE1 is required for accD transcript editing and CAC3 is another ACCase subunit, but the paper does not demonstrate direct physical protein-protein interactions involving AccD.

Omics_Data.reason: The paper reports GC-MS fatty acid profiling and provides no public accession for omics data.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are in Results: rare1 mutants abolishing accD-C794 editing, edited accD overexpression, heat phenotyping, ACCase assay, FA profiling, malonyl-CoA rescue, and TEM.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Although framed as implication, the paper directly shows edited accD overexpression enhances heat tolerance and increases seed FA content, supporting engineering value.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: These points faithfully summarize explicit Results and background statements in the paper.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Discussion explicitly states C794 editing causes an S265L substitution and is dispensable under normal temperature but critical under heat stress.

corrections.reason: No EC number appears in the paper.

Source_Species.reason: RARE1 studied here is from Arabidopsis.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The experimental species is Arabidopsis thaliana.

Applied_Species.reason: All RARE1 experiments were performed in Arabidopsis.

Regulator_Type.reason: RARE1 is explicitly described as a PPR protein required for accD-C794 RNA editing.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper repeatedly calls RARE1 a PPR protein/editing factor.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: RARE1 controls editing of a chloroplast accD transcript, supporting chloroplast localization; this is also consistent with cited prior work.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: The study links RARE1-mediated accD editing to ACCase activity and fatty acid biosynthesis.

Regulation_Mode.reason: RARE1 acts via post-transcriptional C-to-U RNA editing of accD at C794.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: The paper directly states RARE1 is responsible for editing the accD-C794 site.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: Loss of RARE1 abolishes accD-C794 editing; thus it promotes editing of accD transcripts.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: The Results link loss of RARE1 to reduced ACCase activity under heat stress, lower C18:1/C18:2, and heat sensitivity.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results directly show lower C18:1 and C18:2 in rare1 mutants.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: C18:1 and C18:2 are fatty acids/UFAs.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper states these UFAs maintain membrane integrity/fluidity and support heat tolerance.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Leaf membrane effects and seed FA measurements are shown in the study overall, but RARE1-specific metabolite accumulation site is not explicitly stated.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: This is the main Results conclusion for rare1 mutants under heat stress.

Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs.reason: Results show RARE1 expression increased with heat treatment.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The value says 'accD transcript', which is not a protein; moreover no protein interaction partner is experimentally shown for RARE1 in this paper.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Figure 3G shows RARE1 expression increases during heat treatment.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Heat-induced expression of RARE1 is directly reported.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Complementation used pRARE1::RARE1-c-Myc, i.e., native RARE1 promoter.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These treatments are all part of the rare1-related experiments in the paper.

Omics_Data.reason: GC-MS fatty acid profiling was performed and no public accession is reported.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed approaches are described and used in the study for RARE1/rare1 analysis.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a reasonable implication from the data, but not directly tested as a breeding strategy in the paper.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: These three points accurately summarize the main Results regarding RARE1.

Other_Important_Info.reason: This characterization is consistent with the study's framing of RARE1 as the core RNA-editing regulator rather than a transcription factor.

corrections.reason: The value says 'accD transcript', which is not a protein; moreover no protein interaction partner is experimentally shown for RARE1 in this paper.
